results.title: Shores of Gulf St Vincent
results.brief_description: Marine life of the reefs at 80, 60 and 30 foot depths representing submerged shorelines in St. Vincent Gulf, South Australia. Shows the dense population and variety of populations found in these temperate waters.

results.title: Shoppingtown. Livingroom
results.brief_description: Two short films by Australian director, David Caesar. “Shoppingtown” (8 mins, 1987) begins with a blank screen and multiple voices on the soundtrack. Next, we are presented by a series of silent moving image portraits of customers, workers and visitors to a large suburban shopping mall. The portraits reveal the humanity behind the “faceless” mob that move around us in suburban space. With “Living Room” (22mins, 1988), Caesar extends the style of “Shoppingtown” to construct a series of portraits of people framed within their homes. From homeless youth in Kings Cross to first home-owners in the Western suburbs to shopkeepers behind their counters, “Living Room” again humanises the everyday. While the people pose for the camera, written text flashes in between portraits to further give us in insight into their thoughts and lives. By interposing video footage of home construction between the filmed sequences, the film also asks us to interrogate the meaning of “home”, “ownership” and “place”.

results.title: Shooting in basketball
results.creator_credit: Wilbur Johns and Encyclopaedia Britannica Films
results.brief_description: Concerntrating on the set-shot, this film uses professional male basketball players to demonstrate stance, aim, trajectory, and the action of the throw, with detailed attention to fingertip control, the inward rotating movement of the hand and arm, and the co-ordination of all pars of the body required for accurates shots.

results.title: Shipyard
results.brief_description: An outstanding early British documentary made by Rotha and sponsored by the Orient line. Remarkable for its fine photography and editing techniques especially the use of combined dissolves and superimposition to develop two associated ideas simultaneously (influenced by early Soviet filmmaking principles). Describes the building of the steamship “Orion” at Barrow-in-Furness and its economic effects on the life of the town, making particularly effective use of the local dialect (the film unit were noted for their attempt to show the working class under a positive light). Written and edited by Paul Rotha; photography by George Pocknall, Frank Bundy, Frank Goodliffe and Harry Rignold.

results.title: Shipwrecked a True Story
results.creator_credit: Bill Bennett, Bill Bennett Productions and Dick Smith Adventure
results.brief_description: On April 15th, 1978, Bill Belcher, lone yachtsman in a race from New Zealand to Australia, was shipwrecked. His self-designed and built craft was wrecked on a coral atoll some 600 kilometres from the coast of Australia. From that time, until his rescue on May 13th, Bill survived in an inflatable rubber life raft. This is the recreated story of his extraordinary experience.

results.title: Ships of the desert
results.brief_description: A journey through the Middle East, from Israel to Egypt. This fascinating adventure takes you through the Judean Desert to the ancient biblical city of Jericho, across the Sinai Desert to the Dead Sea, then to the Gulf of Aqaba to scuba dive amongst the wonderful coral reefs and on to Eilat. After Jerusalem, “the focus of the law of God and the hatred of Man”, we visit a Kibbutz and travel along the Suez Canal to Cairo to marvel at the pyramids on the banks of the Nile. We then travel to Aswan, to see the incredible Aswan Dam and Abu Simbel then on to Luxor, The Valley of the Kings, and the “Tomb of Tutankhamen”.
